claim: The Currier A and B sub-corpora encode the same underlying language but use two structurally distinct homophonic cipher tables, each mapping plaintext letters to different glyph sets, which explains why the A/B vocabularies share Zipf-law structure but differ in high-frequency word forms.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show different top word distributions yet both obey Zipf scaling (exponent ~0.89)
High belief scores (0.843â€“0.918) consistently support two scribes using two distinct cipher tables on the same underlying language
Word-initial and word-final glyph positional constraints are present in both sub-corpora, suggesting a shared grammatical structure beneath the cipher
evidence_against: If the tables are truly distinct, cross-corpus bigram transitions should show near-zero overlap, but some overlap is observed
Hapax ratio of 70.1% is higher than expected for a homophonic system with a fixed table, which should reduce type diversity
test: Split corpus into Currier A and B; compute pairwise Jaccard similarity of top-200 word types and of word-initial/final glyph frequency vectors between the two sub-corpora; then test whether a shared underlying vocabulary can be recovered by aligning each sub-corpus's high-frequency words to a common rank-ordered list.
test_metric: Jaccard similarity of top-200 word types between A and B; Pearson correlation of their word-initial glyph frequency vectors
test_threshold: Jaccard < 0.15 and glyph-vector correlation > 0.7 would confirm structurally distinct tables over the same language; Jaccard > 0.4 would suggest the same table (or same dialect).

reasoning: If confirmed, this narrows the cipher search to a two-table homophonic system and eliminates single-table substitution; if denied, it supports a genuine bi-lingual or bi-dialectal explanation for the A/B split.
